title: Poly hive vs wooden hive
subtitle: Insulation, maintenance, cost, and handling compared between polystyrene and cedar hives.
excerpt: Both polystyrene and cedar hives will keep a healthy colony of bees alive and productive. The right choice depends on your priorities around cost, durability, insulation, and long-term maintenance. This guide lays out the main differences without recommending one over the other.
sourceNotes: BBKA Hive Type Comparison Notes (2023). Thornes and Abelo manufacturer specifications. Research on polystyrene hive thermal performance, Journal of Apicultural Research, various 2010 to 2020. Ted Hooper, Guide to Bees and Honey (1976), hive construction chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A beehive made from expanded polystyrene, sometimes called a poly hive. EPS stands for expanded polystyrene. The material has high thermal insulation, is light, and does not rot. It requires no painting but is vulnerable to physical damage and degradation from UV if left unpainted.

The most widely used hive type in the UK. A square box design holding 11 British Standard frames in the brood box. Both cedar and polystyrene versions are made to the same internal dimensions so frames and other parts are interchangeable between manufacturers.

A sticky resinous substance collected by bees from tree buds and bark. Bees use it to seal gaps, glue frames to boxes, and coat the interior of the hive. Propolis bonds strongly to wood and polystyrene. Cedar frames tend to accumulate less propolis buildup than older wooden equipment.

content.text: In the UK, the choice is almost always between a cedar wooden hive and an
. Both are most commonly built to the

internal dimensions, which means brood frames, supers, crown boards, and roofs are interchangeable between them. You can run a poly brood box on a cedar floor with a cedar roof. Many beekeepers do exactly this.

Polystyrene hives have a much higher thermal insulation value than 22 mm cedar boards. In cold climates, colonies in poly hives use noticeably less stores over winter because they spend less energy heating the hive. In a UK context the practical difference is real but modest: a well-positioned cedar hive under a hedge does not require dramatically more winter stores than a poly hive in the same spot. The benefit of poly is most obvious in exposed upland sites or during hard winters.

Cedar hives, oiled or painted annually, last 20 to 30 years. The wood weathers well and can be repaired. Joints can be re-glued and boxes sanded flat when they warp. Cedar does not chip, crack under impact, or degrade in UV. It is also easier to clean and sterilise after disease because it can be scorched with a blowtorch.

Polystyrene hives do not rot. They need no annual oiling. But they are vulnerable to physical damage. A dropped poly brood box chips and cracks. Woodpeckers can punch holes through a poly wall faster than through cedar. Bees can chew damage at corners over time. Paint the outside with masonry or exterior emulsion to protect it from UV and reduce the attraction to woodpeckers. Once chipped, repair is possible with polystyrene filler but the box is never as structurally sound as before.

A full poly brood box weighs significantly less than the equivalent cedar box. This matters when moving hives, lifting supers, or if you manage several hives alone. A poly brood box full of honey and bees weighs about 30 kg; the same box in cedar weighs 5 to 8 kg more. Over a season's worth of inspections the lighter weight is a real practical benefit.

in all hive types. On cedar it bonds firmly but scrapes off cleanly with a hive tool. On polystyrene it adheres more aggressively and can pull small chunks of foam away when you scrape. Scorching poly to sterilise it is not possible, which limits disease decontamination options. Poly hive parts can be soaked in a washing-soda solution but cannot be sterilised by heat in the same way that cedar can.

attrs.title: Starting out: which to buy first
attrs.body: Many experienced beekeepers run a mix of both. If cost is the main constraint, a poly hive is usually less expensive than equivalent cedar and performs well. If you value longevity and repairability, cedar is the long-term choice. Buy National dimensions in either case so everything is interchangeable as your apiary grows.

content.text: Poly hives: better insulation, lighter, lower starting cost, no rotting. They are harder to sterilise, chip under impact, and need painting against UV. Cedar hives: heavier, higher starting cost. They can be scorched to sterilise, repair well, last decades with basic upkeep, and handle rough treatment better.
